title: Physical Perimeter — After-Hours Badge Swipes Without Matching Shift

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.7.1 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.7.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.21: Physical Perimeter — After-Hours Badge Swipes Without Matching Shift.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.21' running on pacs:access, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=physical sourcetype=pacs:access earliest=-30d result=granted
| lookup shift_schedule.csv badge_id day_of_week OUTPUT shift_start, shift_end
| eval tod=strftime(_time,"%H:%M")
| where tod < shift_start OR tod > shift_end
| stats count by location, badge_id
description: Flags access to secured areas outside scheduled shift windows for perimeter control monitoring.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes physical and sourcetypes pacs:access. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Physical Perimeter — After-Hours Badge Swipes Without Matching Shift. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
implementation: (1) Populate shift schedules; (2) guard tour exceptions; (3) SOC physical security watchlist. Indexes required: `index=physical`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=pacs:access`; Lookups: `shift_schedule`, `shift_schedule.csv`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: PACS sourcetype.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `pacs:access`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Populate shift schedules; (2) guard tour exceptions; (3) SOC physical security watchlist.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=physical sourcetype=pacs:access earliest=-30d result=granted
| lookup shift_schedule.csv badge_id day_of_week OUTPUT shift_start, shift_end
| eval tod=strftime(_time,"%H:%M")
| where tod < shift_start OR tod > shift_end
| stats count by location, badge_id
```

Understanding this SPL

**Physical Perimeter — After-Hours Badge Swipes Without Matching Shift (A.7.1)** — Flags access to secured areas outside scheduled shift windows for perimeter control monitoring.

Documented **Data sources**: `pacs:access`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): PACS sourcetype. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: physical; **sourcetype**: pacs:access. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=physical, sourcetype=pacs:access, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **tod** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Filters the current rows with `where tod < shift_start OR tod > shift_end` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by location, badge_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Heatmap, Table
knownFalsePositives: Incidents may bunch during major events, vulnerability bulletin response, or controlled exercises.
